tossups.question: A member of this family wrote a play in which Mr. Marshall declares the title “Modern Sentiment” “The wish to please!” An author who married into this family “pleads old magna charta on her side” to advocate for female writers in a play about Louisa Medway’s escape from an arranged marriage, The Discovery. In addition to Alicia Le Fanu and Frances, a member of this family wrote a play in which a hiding woman is called “a little French milliner” by a man who lies to Mr. (*) Stanley. Sir Oliver visits Joseph and Charles Surface in that play by a member of this family about the gossipmonger Lady Sneerwell. For 10 points, name this literary family that included the author of The School for Scandal, Richard Brinsley.
tossups.question_sanitized: A member of this family wrote a play in which Mr. Marshall declares the title "Modern Sentiment" "The wish to please!" An author who married into this family "pleads old magna charta on her side" to advocate for female writers in a play about Louisa Medway's escape from an arranged marriage, The Discovery. In addition to Alicia Le Fanu and Frances, a member of this family wrote a play in which a hiding woman is called "a little French milliner" by a man who lies to Mr. (*) Stanley. Sir Oliver visits Joseph and Charles Surface in that play by a member of this family about the gossipmonger Lady Sneerwell. For 10 points, name this literary family that included the author of The School for Scandal, Richard Brinsley.
tossups.answer: the Sheridan family [prompt on Le Fanu before mentioned] (the play in the first line is Alicia Sheridan Le Fanu’s Sons of Erin)
tossups.answer_sanitized: the Sheridan family [prompt on Le Fanu before mentioned] (the play in the first line is Alicia Sheridan Le Fanu's Sons of Erin)

tossups.question: In a novel by this author, a maid describes a recently arrived, abnormally tall French governess as a sinister Little Red Riding Hood, while her pupil thinks of her as an “Eluesinian princess on [a] vase.” That character created by this author, Madame de la Rougiere, hears the ghost of Lady Rachel shortly after arriving at the Derbyshire estate of Knowl. In another novel by this author, the doctor Sturk is attacked by a murderer who then pays for the former’s surgery to throw others off his trail. An image of a Swedenborgian father pacing through the light and dark bands of his library opens a novel by this author narrated by his daughter, the adolescent Maud Ruthyn. In a novel by this author, Mr. Mervyn buries the body of his executed father Lord Dunoran in the title place near Dublin, all while trying to clear his name by catching the real killer, Dangerfield. The suspicious suicide of a creditor, possibly at the hands of the gambling title relative, drives the plot of that novel by this author, which is often considered the first (*) locked room mystery. For 10 points, name this Anglo-Irish author of the gothic novels The House by the Churchyard and Uncle Silas, as well as the lesbian vampire tale Carmilla.

tossups.question: A Union general with this first name was the father-in-law of Jeb Stuart and authored an 1862 Cavalry Tactics Manual that was used by both sides in the Civil War. Another Union general with this first name who ironically claimed “The Rebel bullet that can kill me has not yet been molded” died, along with Isaac Stevens, at the 1862 Battle of Chantilly; that man with this first name was the nephew of Stephen Kearny. Forces under another general with this first name killed (*) Jeb Stuart at the Battle of Yellow Tavern just outside Richmond and defeated Jubal Early at the Battles of Cedar Creek and Third Winchester. That man with this first name destroyed the Confederate breadbasket by burning much of the Shenandoah Valley. For 10 points, give this first name of the holder of the title General of the Army with the last name Sheridan.
tossups.question_sanitized: A Union general with this first name was the father-in-law of Jeb Stuart and authored an 1862 Cavalry Tactics Manual that was used by both sides in the Civil War. Another Union general with this first name who ironically claimed "The Rebel bullet that can kill me has not yet been molded" died, along with Isaac Stevens, at the 1862 Battle of Chantilly; that man with this first name was the nephew of Stephen Kearny. Forces under another general with this first name killed (*) Jeb Stuart at the Battle of Yellow Tavern just outside Richmond and defeated Jubal Early at the Battles of Cedar Creek and Third Winchester. That man with this first name destroyed the Confederate breadbasket by burning much of the Shenandoah Valley. For 10 points, give this first name of the holder of the title General of the Army with the last name Sheridan.
tossups.answer: Philip [accept Philip St. George Cooke, Philip Kearny, or Philip Sheridan]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Philip [accept Philip St. George Cooke, Philip Kearny, or Philip Sheridan]

tossups.question: This author’s sanitized version of John Vanbrugh’s The Relapse inspired an adaptation of that play spanning from 1800 to the present day by Alan Ayckbourn. George Colman wrote a rhyming epilogue to a play by this author, in which a man who claims to be the poor Mr. Stanley also pretends to be a moneylender with the help of Moses. In that play by this author of A Trip to Scarborough, Oliver Surface disguises himself for his nephews, the wards of a man whose affairs are meddled with by Snake and Lady Sneerwell. In another play by this author, Sir Lucius O’Trigger almost duels Jack Absolute over a woman named Lydia until he realizes that he was actually sending love letters to Mrs. Malaprop. For 10 points, name this playwright of The School for Scandal and The Rivals.

tossups.question: In one of this author’s plays, characters discussing the work of a vain writer based on Richard Cumberland are interrupted by an Italian man with an interpreter who can only translate into French. This author created a cunning maid who earns tips by delivering love notes, but misleads a man as to the identity of “Delia.” In a play by this author, a rehearsal of Mr. Puff’s play The Spanish Armada is attended by Mr. Sneer and Mr. Dangle. In a play by this author, Jack Absolute pretends to be “Ensign Beverley” to woo Lydia Languish, whose guardian often says phrases like “he is the very pineapple of politeness.” That constantly misspeaking character created by this author is the source of the term “malapropism.” For 10 points, name this Irish playwright of The Critic and The Rivals.

tossups.question: In one play by this author, an interpreter who only speaks French repeatedly tries and fails to translate for a family who only speaks Italian. In another play by this author, a man causes his lover to leave in tears after he confronts her about singing and dancing without his knowledge. A George Villiers play mocking John Dryden inspired a play by this author in which the title character insults the vain (*) Fretful Plagiary, then attends a rehearsal of Puff’s play The Spanish Armada. This author wrote a play in which a character masquerading as “Ensign Beverley” is revealed to his lover by a woman who prefers Anthony Absolute, calling him “the very pine-apple of politeness.” For 10 points, name this Irish satirist who created the misspeaking Mrs. Malaprop in his play The Rivals.
tossups.answer: Richard Sheridan [or Richard Brinsley Sheridan] (The play in the first and third sentences is The Critic.)

Mr. Dangle and Mr. Sneer attend one of these events organized by Mr. Puff in a comedy by Richard Brinsley Sheridan. In another work, Philostrate advises a man not to attend one of these events during which a man says “die, die, die, die, die.” A man holds up fingers to represent a crack in a wall during a “tedious and brief” one of these events organized by “rude mechanicals” like Snout and Quince, in which two lovers in Babylon commit suicide. For 10 points, Act 5 Scene 1 of A Midsummer Night’s Dream features Nick Bottom’s performance in an example of what sort of work, titled Pyramus and Thisbe?
tossups.answer: a play performance [or a drama performance; or a masque performance; accept play-within-a-play; prompt on performance by asking “of what sort of work?”] (The Sheridan comedy is The Critic.)

This author’s debut play, set in the town of Bath, failed due to the poor performance of the character Sir Lucius (*) O’Trigger. The second act of a play by this author features many comments from Mr. Sneer and Mr. Dangle, who are watching a rehearsal of the tragedy The Spanish Armada. In a play by this author, Charles Surface throws down the screen covering Lady Teazle, whom her husband and Charles had believed to be a French milliner. This author of The Critic wrote a play about Jack Absolute’s pursuit of Lydia Languish, the niece of Mrs. Malaprop. For 10 points, name this author of The School for Scandal and The Rivals.

For 10 points, name this Union cavalry commander who burned the Shenandoah Valley.

bonuses.leadin: Two days before this play was set to open, its lead actor, Thomas King, locked its playwright into a room with food, wine, and writing implements and forced him to finish the script. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play in which King played the dramatist Puff. In one scene in this play, Sneer points out there’s no reason Sir Walter Raleigh “should be so communicative,” poking fun at exposition-laden dialogue.

The Critic is by this author. This playwright and serial procrastinator wrote “Finished at last, Thank God!” on the last page of The School for Scandal, which he finished only after rehearsals had already started.

Sheridan completed his wildly popular play about this man during its opening night. Based on a work by August von Kotzebue, Sheridan’s play depicts this man’s sparing of Rolla and his duplicitous mistress, Elvira.
answers: Francisco Pizarro {On opening night, Sheridan quickly wrote the play’s final act upstairs while the play went on downstairs, and he would run down every ten minutes to drop off a new batch of dialogue.}

bonuses.leadin: This man’s reforms include removing the then standard discount for audience members who showed up late or left a play early. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this English actor and longtime manager of the Theatre Royal at Drury Lane. Supposedly, the expression “break a leg!” came about after he literally did so during a performance of Richard III.
Garrick is also credited with championing some women writers, such as staging this author’s of The Discovery and The Dupe. Her last play A Trip to Bath remained unperformed by Garrick.

Garrick also began the practice of imposing fines on actors who skipped dress rehearsals, including fining Charles Dibdin who skipped those of his own opera, The Padlock. The Padlock’s libretto was written by this Irishman, who shares his name with a pseudonym used by Jonathan Swift to author astrological predictions.

parts: Name this animal that was hunted relentlessly in an effort to exterminate the Plains Indians and make way for the Transcontinental Railroad.

This general massacred the Cheyenne along the Washita River as part of the buffalo extermination campaign. This man also lost a battle where the only surviving member of his regiment was a horse named Comanche.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about collections of Gothic tales, for 10 points each:
parts: A playwright from this country included “The Surgery Room” in a set of this country’s “Gothic Tales.” Lafcadio Hearn made a collection of this country’s ghost stories, and an author from this country wrote detective fiction under an Edgar Allan Poe-inspired pen name.
This novella published in the collection In a Glass Darkly is presented as part of the notebook of Dr. Hesselius and concerns the supernatural experiences of Laura and General Spielsdorf.
Miss Malin Nat-Og-Dag (“NAHT-oh-DAH”) compares herself to Scheherazade seeing the dawn break at the end of “The Deluge at Norderney,” the first story in this Danish author’s Seven Gothic Tales.

answers: Laura Sheridan [or Laura Sheridan; do not accept “Mrs. Sheridan,” who is Laura’s mother, or “Laurie”, who is Laura’s brother]
Katherine Mansfield Beauchamp [or Katherine Beauchamp; or Katherine Murry; or Lili Heron]
the band of musicians at the garden party

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This character holds a piece of bread and butter while supervising the hanging of a marquee by four workmen, and is horrified when a delivery man from Godber's bakery tells of a man who fell off his horse. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this sister of Jose, a girl who is often complimented on her black hat trimmed with gold daisies and a long velvet ribbon. She delivers a basket of food to the Scott residence.
This New Zealand short story author wrote about Laura Sheridan's concern for the recently-deceased Mr. Scott in "The Garden Party".
In Mansfield's "The Garden Party," this group of people is described as "too like frogs for words" by Kitty Maitland, due to their green coats. Laura begs her father to give these people drinks.
